displayTitle: 2026-06-02 - Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee - International Treaty Examination of the Free Trade Agreement between the Government of New Zealand and the Government of the Republic of India
committee: Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee
topic: International Treaty Examination of the Free Trade Agreement between the Government of New Zealand and the Government of the Republic of India
description: Tuesday 2 June 2026 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee International Treaty Examination of the Free Trade Agreement between the Government of New Zealand and the Government of the Republic of India Hearing of evidence Submitters list: New Zealand International Business Forum Felicity Roxburgh Sophie Tauwehe Tamati Stephen Franks Dianne Downward Voices for Freedom Claire Deeks, Co-Founder Katie Ashby-Koppens, Head of Legal Sam Brosnan Te Taumata (Maori Trade Advisory) Trust Hone McGregor Export New Zealand and Business New Zealand Joshua Tan, Executive Director Tracey Wignall Wayne Widdowson Susan Dyson Nga Toki Whakarururanga Prof Jane Kelsey Jane McKenzie
descriptionSummary: International Treaty Examination of the Free Trade Agreement between the Government of New Zealand and the Government of the Republic of India. New Zealand International Business Forum. Felicity Roxburgh

chunks.text: Uh, good morning, Felicity. Good morning. Tena tatou katoa. All right. Yes, we can hear you and we can see you. It's a good start, and I'll hand straight over to you. Cheers. Wonderful. Well, good morning, everybody. Thank you so much for the opportunity to, um, talk to you on behalf of the New Zealand International Business Forum, who some of you will be, um, familiar with. I'm the new-ish executive director of the International Business Forum, replacing Steven Jacobi about 10 months ago. This is my first time talking to the select committee,

um, in this role, so it's wonderful to have this opportunity. Um, so, uh, you would've all read our submission. The International Business Forum is a group of 22, 22 members. We h- are mostly goods exporters, and we represent, um, a very significant, um, range of exporters and approximately $50 billion worth of goods exports in total.

We are supportive of the India FTA, um, and we have worked, um, including with some members of this committee along the years to deepen the relationship with India, um, and you know, sort of bring this agreement forward to fruition. I wanted to just say three things about it, and then I'm very happy to take any questions. So, um, the first thing I wanted to say is that we do consider the agreement to be commercially meaningful for our members, um, in particular for kiwis, apples, pears, forestry, lamb and sheep meat, those, and seafood.

Those are all, um, members of the New Zealand International Business Forum. And we think it's commercially meaningful because of the good tariff reductions on our exports. We were paying very high tariffs into the Indian market, um, and also just because of the growth opportunity with India. So as we put in the submission, India, um, you know, is on a huge growth trajectory, and that we sort of need to be part of that growth story if we're going to, um, take advantage of, um, you know, the opportunities there. W- our growth to our trade to India

was quite modest, has been quite modest, and in fact, um, reducing in recent years. So, um, in t- in 2024, only 718 million, um, in trade and exports. So when you think of the size of the opportunity, the, um, consumers, um, and, um, you know, our high quality premium products, it's really quite modest.

So, uh, l- many of our members will be making submissions, so I won't talk to the sort of specific areas, but I wanted to talk a little bit more to the strategic piece for us. So in the submission I put that, you know, we're living in a really uncertain global environment, as you all well know, and that there's huge disruption out there. Rising protectionism, geopolitical tension, supply chain disruption.

We're expecting to hear any day on the, you know, American 301 tariffs. Um, we, um, are dealing with, you know, export controls in some of our key markets and, um, we've seen, you know, f- trade being used more as a kind of tool for economic security.

So in that environment, it's really important that our exporters have, um, options, and the, that free trade agreements in their way, you know, they reinforce the rules. So it's g- um, very heartening to see a large and a small country committing to a rules-based, um, agreement, and that for us, having free trade agreements is one of the few insurance policies and one of the key insurance policies that we have against that, um, geopolitical volatility.

So that's, y- you know, I'm happy to talk about that more if you have questions, but building and diversification, um, looking at supply chain choke points, um, having more predictability, that's all really important for New Zealand's business, um, for our exporters. The other thing I just wanted to mention is that, you know, for the International Business Forum, bipartisanship or gro- cross-party support is really important to us. We've worked, um, across both neighbor and National, and had really good engagements in the past with other political parties as well about the importance

of trade. Um, and you know, the, the, the Labor Party in particular has worked, um, on this over many years, a- as has National with its, um, you know, ambitious approach to bring this to fruition. So that's why we signed the Back the Deal letter that, you know, for us, one in four jobs in New Zealand depends on trade.

Um, it creates wealth, jobs, puts more money in the pockets of, um, of everyday New Zealanders. So we, um, yeah, are keen to see that cross-party support hold. So perhaps I'll stop there. I'm happy to take questions with my remaining five minutes. All right. Very good. Have we got questions for Felicity in the NZ International Business Forum?

First question, Dr. Lawrence Yun. Kia ora. Uh, thank you, Chair. Thank you, Felicity, and congratulations. Um, just a question on the last thing that you kind of mentioned. I'm, I'm interested to know, how do you think this agreement would benefit everyday New Zealanders?

Yeah. Well, just what I said that, you know, one in four jobs in New Zealand depends on trade, that our members are really significant, um, employers. That, um-They employ and train many New Zealanders, and in order to continue to be successful overseas, they need those options, that diversification, that they can go into other markets.

So over time, we expect trade with India to grow and that to create more opportunities. Can I just check with a follow-up question? I understand one in four jobs in here are tied into trade, but I guess that's trade in general. Mm-hmm. What is the additional opportunity if you-- It's fine if NZIBF hasn't actually done ...

I'll be keen to know exactly with this particular agreement, not including existing trade relations with India, what are some of the potential-- Have you done any analysis on what potential jobs could be created as a result of this? Particularly when you're looking at urban centers and et cetera, where there seems to be a higher rate of unemployment.

Mm-hmm. I think it's a good question. We haven't modeled specific jobs. The only thing I would say is that we consider the national interest analysis to be quite conservative, that actually when we look at the modeling that was done for the China FTA and the EU FTA, in fact, that modeling was quite conservative compared to the amount of export earning that came. With the EU agreement, I think $3 billion, nobody really expected that much success with the EU.

Mm-hmm. Cool. Thanks. Next question, Dr., sorry, Honorable Damien O'Connor. Look, thank you very much, and apologies for not being on video. Look, and I missed it, unfortunately, got disconnected. But Felicity, thank you for your submission. What level of investment have you had indications from your members that is likely to go into India, given the commitment to promote, and I guess, the honest assessment that the Indians will be watching this very, very closely.

How much do you think will come from your members? Mm-hmm. Yeah, the first thing I'd say is that we, having read the text, believe this is a commitment by the government to promote, not a commitment by our investors necessarily to invest. That said, there are cooperation activities already, as you know, in kiwis and apples.

So I guess on the investment, we accept the assurance of the government that the Indian side understands that this is a commitment to promote, and that it's not a target per se. And we sort of accept that they will have a way to measure that. I think in general terms, as you know, Damien, investment does flow from trade. The more that we trade, the more that we invest.

So we would expect our investment to grow, but we haven't modeled specific investment that we expect from our members. Tim Costley. Yeah, just thinking about that, because I'm thinking back 20-odd years to when we first had the China Free Trade Agreement, and I think there were a number of similar questions, and I think we can understand why people might have that, because it hasn't taken effect yet, so we haven't seen that gain.

But we've had some people come on here and say, "Oh, we don't think we should do it." But they don't think we're better off without it. Mm-hmm. So I'm just wondering, what's your message to people like that? We heard the question before, how is this going to create a job in urban centers? Actually, there's massive growth in the regions, and sometimes, we need to move to where the opportunity is. We need to be a bit agile.

What's your message, though, to people that maybe are thinking this is, I don't know, they have questions about the future and if it will really grow. Mm-hmm. Just that this is a really important strategic play for New Zealand in the future, that we need to be part of the Indian growth story. That it provides us options, but that India is not just any port in a storm, that we do see this kind of massively burgeoning middle class, and that we expect that there will be a desire for our high-quality premium products. What's the counterfactual?

We wait another 20 years for a better deal? We're already at a competitive disadvantage from some of our key partners, particularly the Aussies in that market, and we really have to be in it to win it at this point. Excellent. All right. That brings us to an end of this session.
Thank you very much for your time, Ms. Roxburgh, on behalf of the New Zealand International Business Forum. Thank you. Thanks for having me. Cheers. Okay, we move now to Sophie Tamati.

Kia ora. Good morning, Ms. Tamati. Sorry, I'm just having difficulty there. There we go. Yes, we've got you now. Excellent. All right. Right, straight over to you. Tuatahi me mihi atu ki a koe te hemana, ki o koutou katoa ngā mema o te komiti. I just want to say thank you for this opportunity to share my thoughts.

It is my first time to write a submission, and indeed, I was so surprised really to receive the email to say I could actually talk to you all. But I'll get to the point. I do want to read a little bit, and maybe you will have questions if there's time left over. My submission focus focuses on my concern with the process used to negotiate this agreement, and whether that process was consistent with the Crown's treaty obligations.In my view-- Sorry, there's this thing that's popped up.

In my view, this agreement highlights the ongoing tension between how trade agreements are negotiated and the Crown's responsibilities under the Treaty of Waitangi. Treaty partnership requires more than just mere consultation after key decisions have been shaped. It requires good faith engagement that is informed and meaningful to influence outcomes, certainly for Maori.

Maori must have access to the information needed to fully engage and make informed decisions that affect their rights and interests. The treaty exception clause allows the Crown to act if necessary once an agreement is in place. It's not a substitute for treaty partnership because it doesn't give Maori the opportunity to shape the agreement from the outset.

New Zealand has committed to supporting the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples, including rights to participate in decision-making and informed consent. But it's unclear whether these were fully met in this agreement.

Precedent is not just set by law. It's also set by what we do repeatedly. If Maori participation is repeatedly limited in negotiating trade agreements, that sets a precedent, and over time that, in my opinion, risks marginalizing the treaty.

The key step now for me is an independent review to check if treaty obligations were met before ratification and identify any gaps to improve future processes. All I want is for the process to respect the treaty, to be fair, and to lead to strong, lasting agreements.

Excellent. Well done, Ms. Tamati. Appreciate your contribution. Now we've got time for a quick question if there are any from members. Excellent. Oh, no, Mr. Chair, but I just wanted to say it was great to-- I didn't agree with 100% of what you said, but I just thought it was great that you took the time to submit and came and chatted today. So well done.
Yes. Thank you. Excellent. Thank you for your time. All right then. Thank you. Okay, we move now to Steven Franks.

Good morning. Good morning, Mr. Franks. We can't see you. Yeah, but we can hear you. I'm hopeful I can change that, but I'll carry on if I can't. Well, we can see an empty chair now. Take off your cloak of invisibility and we'll be fine. I'll move it one way or the other, but I'll be there.

Okay. Ah, yes, there we go. That's good. Very good. Thank you. Okay. Crack on. My concern, I should say, welcome the free trade agreement with India. I'm the chairman of the Forest Owners Levy Trust, and of course, Forestry is delighted that this achievement has got as far as it has as quickly as it has.

But I am really concerned that there are people within the Ministry of Foreign Affairs who have done, again, what has often been done with international agreements. They have embarked on some sort of frolic to achieve domestic political aims that have absolutely nothing to do with free trade of reaching an agreement with India.

And the inclusion of UNIDROIT and indeed the elevation of it is a disgrace, and I ask, I really hope that the ACT Party would see it as a sufficiently important matter to send a message that officials or even ministers should not pull this kind of stunt by saying they won't agree with it until that damage is nullified. This is why people mistrust free trade agreements.

They believe, it's just as Jane Kelsey has always said, that they are exploited for domestic political advantage. And in this case is a brilliant example where UNIDROIT has absolutely no relevance. India, indeed, has reserved its position, no relevance to the India Free Trade Agreement.

And the inclusion of it is really what I discovered when I was an MP and sitting in your shoes over two terms, that one simply could not trust foreign affairs officials to be there without them trying to screw the scrum. And in this case, UNIDROIT is only in a declarationNew Zealand is a signed up covenanted party to the United Nations Convention for the Elimination of all forms of Racial Discrimination, which therefore has a superior legal status to UNIDROIT.

But there are New Zealand judges and there are people in the New Zealand Wellington establishment trying to elevate UNIDROIT above the UNCERD, above the Convention for the Elimination of all Forms of Racial Discrimination. Which is, if you care to read the two, you'll see that they are clearly inconsistent, and the earlier one has a higher legal status, which some of the New Zealand judges are trying to erode.

I'm happy to answer any questions, but it's a plea to MPs to stand up to make sure that this kind of stunt doesn't just get pulled on you again in hundreds of pages of documents to have something like this, using free trade process to achieve covert domestic political gain.

All right. We have a question from the Honorable Damien O'Connor. Look, thank you, Steven, and thankfully, Parliament has more wisdom than the ACT Party, so it takes a more collective approach. Can I just ask of you, trade agreements are for the purposes of benefit to the domestic people in the trading nations, not just for an ideological signing of an agreement. Do you agree with that purpose?

No. Trade agreements are for reducing the barriers that countries erect against other people's products. They- Yeah, but what are the purposes of removing those barriers? Well, Damien, it's idle to ask me rhetorical questions. Of course, they're for benefit. But that doesn't mean that you take- Well-... a highly contentious piece of international legal engineering and stick it into your trade agreement because people want the trade agreement.

I- They'll then agree to forgo debate on the domestic engineering. I was just challenging your view that trade agreements are not for domestic purposes, and I would contest that. Indeed, that's the objective. It is to benefit the people of a nation involved in the agreement.

Well, that's never in doubt, but you don't sign it unless it's to benefit the people of the nation. The question is whether sticking a reference on a matter that is highly contentious internally where New Zealanders should, and New Zealand Parliament should get a chance to debate the detail. Sticking that into treaties has been a time-honored way to try and evade local political process, and it's sneaky, and it means that these things get discredited.
All right. Thank you very much for your time this morning, Mr. Franks. Appreciate it. Okay. We move now to Dianne Downward.
Good morning, Dianne.
Okay, we can see you, Dianne.
Sorry, we can't hear you very well. There's sound there, but it's very quiet.
Seems to be a volume issue. Got IT support there?
I don't know. Can you call me on my phone?
No, we're not able to pick you up, unfortunately. There is a sound there, so it's not a mute issue, but it's a volume issue. Can you hear me at all then if I talk loud?
Perhaps we might look at heading to our next submitter if they're available and

just get an indication from the clerk if the next submitter is available. They are. So look, Dianne, perhaps take a moment while we're hearing from the next submitter to see if you can rectify the sound issue, and we can try to bring you in after that. Oh, thank you. Thank you.
Okay. Right, now we have Voices for Freedom.

Good morning. Excellent. We can hear you. Great. Thank you. Because I have no tech support. Very good. Okay. Over to you, Katie. Thank you. Thank you, chair and members. My name is Katie Ashby-Coppins. I'm here this morning on behalf of Voices for Freedom as the head of legal. Voices for Freedom is a grassroots New Zealand organization started by three moms in 2020.

Today, there are more than 100,000 supporters behind us, with more than 40,000 active across 100 local groups spread throughout the country.Today I've kept to our code of conduct this morning, respectful, measured, and grounded in the documents. I want to start out by saying we are not against trade, and we're not against India, and we're not suggesting that New Zealand should isolate itself from the world.

What we are concerned about is something much simpler. A decision of this magnitude was made before New Zealanders were even asked whether they agreed with it, and that really is my first point. Before turning to the detail, I have one fundamental question for this committee. What provisions of this agreement, if any, could still be amended, renegotiated, or declined as a result of concerns raised through this process?

If the answer is very few, then many New Zealanders will ask the simple question: If the agreement cannot meaningfully be changed, what exactly are we being consulted on today? As once this agreement enters into force, much of it becomes very difficult to unwind. If we get it wrong, we simply can't amend a policy or elect a different government and move in a different direction.

We are left with two choices: live with the consequences or withdraw from the agreement altogether, which on the international stage is terribly embarrassing. What is not how many New Zealanders would expect important and far-reaching national decisions to be made. We are therefore asking this committee to recommend that New Zealand not ratify this agreement in its current form. At the very least, we ask that ratification be delayed until there is proper public consultation on the long-term immigration, investment, and sovereignty consequences

of the commitments being made. Our concerns can be summarized now under three headings. So that's me turning to the documents. First, there was no meaningful public consultation before signing. Second, the agreement contains immigration and investment commitments that future governments may find difficult or impossible to reverse.

And third, the economic benefits appear most modest compared with the concessions being given away. Turning to the economics, the government's own modeling projects gains of approximately $400 million per year on average through to 2037. That number does sound like a large number, but in the context of New Zealand's economy, it's actually quite modest. It's equivalent to a fortnight of government borrowing.

Even more importantly, much of the projected export growth is not new trade at all. According again to the modeling, around three-quarters of the increase in exports to India comes from trade being redirected away from markets we already supply. So while the headline numbers sound impressive, the practical gains appear considerably smaller than New Zealanders might assume.

Again, those gains we must consider what is being given away. One of the greatest concerns raised by our supporters is immigration. The student annex provides a clear example. New Zealand has agreed not to impose numerical caps on Indian students, but we do not currently have such caps now, so the issue is what changes tomorrow?

The issue is what options are surrendered for the future by the signing of this agreement in its current form. Around the world, countries such as Australia, Canada, and United Kingdom are currently reviewing and restricting international student numbers because of concerns around housing, infrastructure, migration pressures, and economic influences.

The practical effect of this provision removes a policy option for future governments, whether that's intended or not. And this is not merely an educational issue. It's also an immigration issue. Government figures show that Indian students transition to permanent residence at a higher rate than any other nationality, just shy of 50%.

The work provisions raise similar concerns. The specialist category within the intra-corporate transferee framework contains no numerical cap, no New Zealand wage floor, and no requirement to test to see whether a New Zealander is available to do the job first. There are, of course, qualification requirements, but they are largely administrative in nature.

Taken together, the student and worker pathways point to one direction, ongoing permanent settlement from a single source country. Whether people support that outcome or oppose it is not really the point. The point is that New Zealanders were never asked. No election was fought on it, no public consultation was conducted on it, yet these commitments would become harder to change than ordinary immigration policy because they would sit inside an international treaty.

That leads directly to our second concern. Many of the most significant commitments in this agreement are difficult to reverse. A future government that wished to cap student numbers or require employers to look for New Zealand workers first would not necessarily be free to do so. Those policy tools are effectively surrendered once the agreement takes effect.

That matters because governments change, public priorities change, economic conditions change, and we do not have a crystal ball for the future. Yet this agreement locks future governments into this decision made today. The investment chapter raises similar concerns. New Zealand has committed to seek approximately $20 billion of additional investment into India over 15 years. That's around $33 billion New Zealand dollars.

India has undertaken no equivalent commitment in return. If New Zealand fails to meet that target, India may suspend tariff concessions obtained by New Zealand exporters. There is no independent arbitrator determining whether that response is justified. There are simply further consultations.The concern is that the agreement reaches beyond tariffs and trade.

It also includes cooperation on payment systems and centralized bank digital currencies. Whether those ideas are good or bad is not my point. My point is that decisions about the future of our money should be debated in public and not discovered in the annexures of a trade agreement. Many New Zealanders will reasonably ask why New Zealand should be committing itself to such a large one-way objective when we face pressing infrastructure, housing, and investment needs at home.

The final concern is whether the agreement represents a balance exchange. At the border, New Zealand removes tariffs on all Indian goods immediately. India doesn't do the same. Approximately 30% of Indian tariff lines remain protected, including sectors of greatest interest to New Zealand, such as dairy and meat.

At the same time, government's own modeling projects significant increases in imports of clothing, textiles, and other manufactured goods from India. These are precisely the industries weak that remain vulnerable in New Zealand. In other words, the gains appear relatively modest, while many of the concessions are permanent. That is why this agreement deserves more scrutiny than it has received. It's not simply about trade, it is about choices, choices that future governments may wish to make, choices that future voters may wish to make, choices that become

significantly harder once they're embedded in this international treaty. So that's where the government's own documents leave us. New Zealand was not consulted before these commitments were weighed. Future governments may find many of the commitments difficult to reverse, and the projected economic gains appear modest relative to concessions being offered.

I think a good example is a child born in New Zealand this morning will not be able to cast their first vote until 2044, yet they'll inherit the consequences of the decisions made today. We therefore ask the committee to recommend that New Zealand not ratify this agreement in its current form, and at the very least, we ask that ratification be delayed until New Zealanders have been given the opportunity to fully understand and debate what is being agreed in their name. Thank you very much.

Right. Thank you very much. Really appreciate that detailed assessment of the FTA. Time for a quick question if members have one. Dr Lawrence, you may. Cool. Thank you, Chair. Thank you so much for your submission, Katie. I just have a quick question on, just so you're aware as well, in terms of the international treaty examination process, the process itself rather than the content, which is part of your submission, is something that the Standing Orders Committee is currently looking at, so standing orders 405 to 408.

And just so you are aware, that might be something for you to keep an eye on. Oh, good. Thank you so much. I appreciate that, Dr Lawrence. Cool. Thank you. All right. Thank you very much for your time this morning, Ms Ashley-Coppins. Appreciate your- Oh. Yes. Wonderful. I think you've got Diane back, so I will look forward to watching her. Good on you, Diane.

Hi. Okay. We can hear you this time, Diane. We'll crack straight into it. Oh, yeah, sorry about that. Good. Yeah. So I'm not against free trade with India, but this agreement comes at a cost in more than just dollars, as many people have already outlined. Much has already been said about the problems of immigration in relation to jobs, in particular tech jobs, although we may need a few more apple pickers.

I will not repeat all the problems that have been mentioned here, but only state that this seems to be robbing our young people of their future here in New Zealand. So something that hasn't been talked about a lot is the Paris Article six of the Paris Agreement being introduced to this trade agreement. As the IPCC has now stated that their SSP5 8.5 is implausible, which according to the dictionary means causing disbelief or it refers to something that is difficult to believe or accept as true.

So the actual standard that has been used to calculate all our climate models is now considered difficult to believe or accept as true. I feel a recalculation is in order, especially since Article 128 of this agreement states it's to be based on the best available science. So data causing disbelief is not the best science available.

Also, considering 50% of our greenhouse gas is from farming, and the Paris Agreement has always stated in Article 2B, that food production shouldn't be threatened, i.e., should not be included in greenhouse gas emissions. But for some reason, successive governments have made farming a focus of our emission reductions.

I think it is prudical that Article 6 of the Paris Agreement is not included in this trade agreement and could be discussed at a future date when everything has been recalculated using the best science available. I want to mention Howie Smith, sorry, the Albana Gowie Smith submission about digital IDs and CBDCs and all the associated implications, and state that I support his submission.

Another highly objectionable element is Article 92, investment promotion, where New Zealand will aim to invest $20 billion US in India, as already been mentioned by many people. And it's also been highlighted, oh, this is just an aim. But because there is also a whole procedure to reach a mutually agreed solution if this is not fulfilled, this is far more than just an aim.So we're investing billions in India, all the while we cannot afford to keep our health professionals, keep police we've trained, build badly needed infrastructure, and all the other issues people

have mentioned. So this also needs to be dropped. Another issue many have mentioned is UNDRIP, which has no place in a trade agreement. India has not asked for this to be included, and indeed, it considers all its citizens indigenous, not favoring one ethnic group over another, and they have many ethnic groups there.

We have the Treaty of Waitangi addressing all the same concerns that are within UNDRIP. So what is the benefit of adding yet another document to be discussed, defined, and interpreted? Adding UNDRIP could have unintended consequences. Maori themselves are not a united people, as some fly the sovereignty flag and others Hei Waka Putanga, and many, many Maori just want to get on with their life, put food on the table, have a roof over their head, and are not concerned with the politics of advancing Maori culture and language.

Therefore, I ask that Article 13.2 be removed completely. It is not needed and could only complicate discussions and processes already taking place under the treaty. I see a larger issue with social cohesion at this time, especially when large numbers of people have come, and will still come from countries culturally different to our own, and will enlarge the growing number of cultural enclaves in our major cities.

Having different cultural norms existing in the same country only causes clashes when the two come into conflict. Uniting around Kiwi values, cultures, laws, and traditions is the glue of a socially cohesive society where everyone understands the same norms.

Without the social norms, it would be like driving on the road without a road code. So thank you. Any questions? All right. Thank you very much for that, Diane. We are at the end of our time, so we will keep going, but appreciate your submission, and thank you for being able to work through those technical issues.

Okay. Sorry. Yeah. Thank you. No problem. Thanks. Okay, we come next to Sam Brosnan. I'm not sure that he's here. So we will move instead to Te Taumata Trust Maori Advisory Committee and Hone McGregor. Right. Hello. Thank you. Yes. Hone McGregor. Kia ora.

Right. Okay. I'll bring straight to you, Hone. Kia ora. Thanks. Yes, we're Te Taumata Maori Trade Advisory Trust. So thanks for the opportunity to appear before the committee today. I've only got a few minutes, so I'd probably like to focus on just four things. Firstly, why Te Taumata supports this agreement, why it matters for Maori exporters in the Maori economy, why we also support the cultural chapter in the agreement, and then lastly about implementation. So, I do just want to start off by just acknowledging all the

work of everybody because we don't do that enough for the ministers, the negotiators, the officials both in New Zealand and over in India to bring this agreement to completion. We understand that trade agreements are never perfect. Every agreement involves a give and take. Every agreement leaves some issues that are unresolved, and the question isn't whether the agreement's perfect actually.

The question is whether it leaves New Zealand, and in our case, Maori exporters and Maori businesses better positioned for the future than we otherwise would be. And of course, our view is that this agreement does do that. Maori, we take a different view on trade. We've been traders for a long time.

Before Aotearoa actually developed this modern New Zealand economy, the Maori were providors of food and timber, produce, and those sorts of things across the Tasman. We had Maori-owned and flagged ships moving to Australia doing trade as early as the 19th century.

So that's really part of this kaupapa of trade and enterprise and relationship building that's been part of our story for the last over 200 years. And just in terms of why Maori should be involved in this. Maori actually aren't niche participants in New Zealand's international trade because today, Maori own or control approximately half of all New Zealand's fishing quota, 40% of the forestry assets, about a third of sheep, beef, and lamb production, 10% of the dairy, and then 10% of kiwifruit production.

And so collectively, these sectors contribute a billion dollars annually to export earnings that is directly connected to these Maori interests, which are then deeply connected to the international market. So when these export markets perform well, Maori communities actually benefit. When international markets are disrupted, as Maori communities, we actually feel those impacts really directly.

And so Maori are actually more exposed to the international trading system than many New Zealanders actually realize. But the long-term view for us, in 2018 to 2023, the Maori economy, that growth really happened because Maori increasingly invested along the value chains to capture more value.

We built brands, and we participated in more export markets. Services, technology, and innovation sectors were part of that investment, and many of Maori entities, the ones that will be participating in export, really consider this as an intergenerational strategy. Wakatu Incorporation, who I'm a chairman of, we actually have a 500-year intergenerational plan called Te Pae Tawhiti.

When you start looking at those kind of horizons, your perspectives actually change. You stop asking about whether it's perfectIn today's context, and we actually start looking at it from, is it based on the right values? Does it create these greater opportunities, this resilience, this prosperity for those future generations and for us?

Te Taumata, this agreement contemplates a mutually prosperous intergenerational relationship. And we heard from Felicity, she did a good summary of these things, why India's important to us. It is a really important long-term geopolitical strategic partner for Aotearoa, but particularly for this Indo-Pacific trading bloc. Yes, it's going to have a really significant growing middle class. It will be the third largest economy.

They will exceed the EU population around things. So the question for us isn't whether India and this FTA matters, the question really is whether New Zealand is positioned inside that growth story or outside of it, and in Te Taumata, we believe that we should be inside it. For those reasons that we already heard about the world becoming more unpredictable, more protectionism, our supply chains are under real pressure. So diversification isn't really just about a growth strategy for all the members of the Māori economy, it's actually a risk management strategy,

it's a resilience strategy, and it gives us that thing that Vangais talked about in his presentation around optionality. So we believe that this FTA provides that optionality. There's some really specific stuff that Māori-owned exporters across the seafood, forestry, and farming, and horticulture systems get, and you've already heard about that stuff. But immediate tariff elimination on sheep meat, significant gains in forestry, seafood exporters tariff reductions.

Wine, so we're the house of Tohu wines at Wakatu Incorporation, the first Māori wine brand in the world, I have to say. 150% tariffs were in India, dropping down to 75%, and then over 10 years down to 25%. That's not fantastic in the beginning, but actually it's really important because it actually creates that level playing field that we heard them talk about between us and Aussie, and the UK, and also the EU.

So that along with kiwifruit, we're the only Māori kiwifruit grower of the South Island. Those are real opportunities. They are practical commercial outcomes for Māori businesses and those communities that own them. The last thing I really wanted to talk about was, which I haven't really heard a lot about, is this cultural chapter.

The cultural trade, traditional knowledge, and economic cooperation chapter in 13. And I think from a Māori perspective, this might be one of the most strategically important parts of the FTA. That chapter explicitly recognizes Māori. It recognizes traditional knowledge as an economic and a cultural asset. It recognizes the importance of protecting traditional knowledge from any misappropriation, and it also recognizes that the implementation should be consistent with Te Tiriti o Waitangi.

Very specifically in that, it actually creates opportunities, and we heard a question from one of the committee members before about what does it give? Well, it actually provides specific opportunities for Rongomai Māori collaboration and research. That traditional medicines partnership with the AYUSH system in India. Indigenous innovation and research partnerships and exchanges, which we have already currently. And then Māori SME development and capability development, and then Māori business missions and commercial

partnerships. I was in India just recently, and we met with one of the chambers of commerce and industry. They had 1.5 million members. This chapter provides SME capability building and partnerships. So that's really important. So if Māori choose to use it well, this chapter will be strategically important for the agreement.

The last thing for me was really just talk about implementation, because I think obviously implementation matters. Trade agreements don't create exports. People create exports. Relationships create exports. Obviously, trust creates exports. So trade agreements just remove barriers, and the real work starts at that ratification. So just, I guess, to leave a message with the government, to get the real value from this FTA, we really think that Māori exporters have to be connected into these trade missions and market development programs, those

investment pathways, this business matching opportunities, and then any future review mechanisms. So traditionally for Māori, our challenge has really been about gaining access, and that's the access to markets, the capital networks, and value chains. This agreement actually doesn't solve all of those challenges, but it does fundamentally improve access, and from a Māori exporter perspective, access matters because every generation that follows should inherit more opportunities than the generation before it.

So for those reason, this gives us more confidence in trade and more certainty, and Te Taumata supports the ratification of the New Zealand India Free Trade Agreement. So kia ora koutou. Happy to answer any questions. Excellent. Thank you very much. There was a question from Vanushi Walters, or? There was. I suspect that the submitters semi answered it, but I might ask it anyway.

There are other submitters who've argued that those sections on traditional knowledge and genetic resources, Māori medicine, aren't sufficient or don't offer sufficient protections. Did you want to respond to that? Yeah, I mean, I don't think those concerns should be dismissed.

There are legitimate conversations to be had about that, particularly around implementation and protection of Māori interests. The difference that Te Taumata is we've been looking at this through the lens of Māori exporters and Māori economic development. And from that perspective, the agreement creates meaningful new access and opportunities while actually preserving the treaty exception.

So we see support around this, in terms of the economic opportunityAnd then also continuing to improve those treaty settings, because we think they're complementary objectives, not really competing ones in this. Thank you. Very good. All right. Thank you very much, Mr.

government agencies need to be working closer with industry, and prioritize practical measures to support exporters wanting to leverage this agreement. It means working with the Indian government to also understand what opportunities there are to invest in India. I think it also, further to your question, depends on what is defined as investment. agritech, education, clean technology. These are areas which can lead to economic growth and better outcomes for the Indian population. So- Can I just-... I guess the question is- Yeah Yeah Can I just check? Because what you are saying is your interpretation. You specified New Zealand interpretation.

I've got a phone going off. Sorry about that. Nice fun little ringtone there. Okay. So a substantial increase in migration to New Zealand will, over time, lead to significant and enduring changes to our nation's demographic profile and social cohesion.

I believe that when countries with markedly different cultural values are involved, it is important to implement stricter controls rather than relaxing themThe FTA may limit New Zealand's ability to regulate student admissions, determine labor market policies, and influence long-term migration outcomes.

For context, there was nearly 48,000 international tertiary students enrolled in New Zealand in 2025, in addition to the many international students at the 20 English language schools. Indian students will be able to also bring their families with them for the duration of their studies, for as long as they can finance it.

While most international students leave on completion of their studies, reports suggest that 47% of Indian students remain in New Zealand and transition to permanent residents. Indian students have the opportunity to work while studying. With unemployment already a concern in New Zealand, this training will intensify competition for entry-level jobs, making it even harder for New Zealanders, including those with disabilities, to find employment as the job market becomes overcrowded.

I mean, more crowded. An example is Domino Pizzas. They're already an accredited employer, and they employ many Indian workers in those entry-level positions. I strongly oppose affirming the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous People, the UNDRIP, in the India FTA. Some New Zealand legal experts warn that this could create a binding international obligation impacting how domestic laws are interpreted.

Courts may treat UNDRIP as authoritative, effectively making it binding in New Zealand law. The India FTA affirms UNDRIP, unlike the UK and EU arrangements or agreements that only note it. So perhaps indicating a deeper commitment. The provision could also act as a constitutional Trojan horse, potentially justifying political change like co-governance through trade policy.

Parliament can only accept or reject the agreement as signed. If approved, UNDRIP's affirmation will be embedded in New Zealand law to the same extent as this FTA. The FTA process raises significant concerns about the New Zealand government's transparency and upholding of democracy, especially given the lack of public debate and domestic ratification on UNDRIP, and could have significant consequences, including the increasing division along racial lines within New Zealand.

There are no anti-corruption mechanisms within this FTA. In India, corruption affects many official processes such as document issuance, university admissions, and academic grading, making it difficult for New Zealand institutions to verify the authenticity of submitted documents and qualifications with certainty.

Additionally, India's manufacturing sector faces widespread counterfeiting across industries including fashion, electronics, pharmaceutical, food, automotive, and agrochemicals, the very products that will gain free range entry to New Zealand. An example of this is from 2025, where 436 Indian nationals used fake documents and support letters regarding previous employment as truck drivers to obtain United Arab Emirates truck driving licenses, and then they used those UAE documents to gain class five licenses in New Zealand fraudulently.

And lastly, the financial cost to New Zealand. New Zealand has agreed to invest $33 billion of New Zealand money in India over the next 15 years, averaging at $2.23 billion per year to help build capacity to compete against New Zealand in global markets.

Yeah. There are not enough guarantees that New Zealand exports will significantly increase profits to New Zealand with this in mind, and India can re-impose tariffs if we do not make these payments. I suspect, especially having heard some recent submissions, that I'm one of very few people outside the MFAT legal team who has read the entire India FTA and understood its implications.

I had planned to be in Wellington to present the submission and discuss this with the Select Committee. Once again, we've got this five-minute nonsense, which presents, sadly, the agreement as a fait accompli and the hearings as a bit of a sham, and I'm really sorry that labor seems to be complicit in that.Let me explain. I have worked for a number of years quite closely with Indian trade officials, and I understand very well where they're coming from.

The agreement is exactly what I would have expected from them. It is asymmetrical. There are minuscule gains in there for New Zealand. There is a focus on what India hopes to achieve from it, but it is also quite possible for India to effectively cancel whatever concessions they have made if either they claim that it has not been delivered or circumstances have changed.

I am aware of and was monitoring the previous negotiations between New Zealand and India, and indeed suggested ways that the problems might have been dealt with, which previous government chose not to do. It would've been possible to have some of those conversations this time. However, the Prime Minister's precipitous statement about when he intended to have this concluded disarmed our negotiators even further than previously. I note, for example, the negotiation

between India and the EU took four years. The one with the UK was over two years to reach an agreement in principle. So there was no surprise, as I said, when finally after the fact and after the signing, I was able to give a close reading to the agreement. Let me just give two examples in the short time I have of major difficulties that emerged in the text that weren't part of the spin that we heard from the government.

The first relates to the tariff rate quotas for apples, kiwifruit, and honey, which are all captive of action plans, which we have not seen because I understand that they've not yet been concluded, or if they have, they've not been released. The action plans are decided in accordance with India's understandable preference to assist India's producers to be able to produce of a quality and in a competitive manner with what New Zealand is planning to export into India under this FTA. It rather reminds me of a previous occasion when there was a New Zealand

export award to exporters of the kiwifruit stock to Chile, which then meant that Chile ended up being a major competitor, not only in Chile, but internationally. That is the intention, as I say, understandably of India. But it's an own goal from New Zealand's side, and it can be after five years with a review and a bit of to and fro, canceled effectively by India deciding that New Zealand has not met its part of the bargain.

There is no dispute mechanism that would apply to this. And indeed, it was done so quickly that there was a major drafting error that I had to point out to the New Zealand MFAT officials to fix in relation to the tariff rate quotas in Article 14.11.

The second example is this bizarre commitment to promote $20 billion US of much needed New Zealand investment into India. For all the language about promote, it is a positive obligation which New Zealand has to show that it has made best endeavors as an international obligation to meet.

India inserted in the previous agreement where it had such a provision with EFTA, a footnote that referred or effectively tied the obligation to the continuation of a GDP growth rate in India of around 9.7% per annum. That footnote and that exit point is not in the New Zealand FTA. What we have instead is an ability on the part of India through a whole series of reviews, five, 10, and 15 years, and then to and fro after that

for India to conclude that New Zealand has not promoted 20 US billion dollars of investment and can effectively unilaterally decide to negate some or a large part of the concessions it's made on tariffs to New Zealand. In addition to that, we have, of course, New Zealand's need for domestic investment which will be obvious to all of the members of the committee.

Sorry, I just had a quick question on that if I could just before we finish up. The Article 9.10, Subclause 3 says that those remedial measures you've just mentioned can stay in place until the investment objective has been achieved. Yes. Is your read of that, that the investment objective is the $20 billion US?

Yes. It is that New Zealand has taken steps actively to promote that quantum of investment into India, yes. Yes, and if India feels that hasn't been done, they can reimpose tariffs, and those tariffs stay in place until the $20 billion is met.

Until New Zealand can convince India thatIt has met its international obligations. And because India will be the one that decides this, and there is no appeal in this chapter, there is no dispute mechanism in this chapter, it is effectively India's unilateral decision how it interprets that.

Okay. So I just have a couple of concluding comments. There are many other flaws in the agreement and in the NIA. There has been a great deal of mischief on immigration. I was appalled to hear the previous witness's evidence. There have been backward steps in relation to the Crown's obligations onto Tiriti o Waitangi.

Ngā Toki Whakarururanga, who you will hear from shortly, had in fact negotiated with MFAT an improved version of the 2001 Treaty of Waitangi exception that was tabled with India. It's not in the final text. That's a really unfortunate development because the 2001 exception still therefore has the many flaws that MFAT has now basically acknowledged by adjusting the new one.

The nonsense about the UN declaration, which actually has if you look at the UAE agreement, which also affirms the UN declaration, this agreement refers to the existing reservations that both countries adopted. That was not even in the UAE one.

Clearly, India's pressure around this, and this will have been from India, is designed to minimize any potential action in relation to the UN declaration. That provision also refers to not duplicating anything in the FTA that is in other fora, such as the United Nations. It makes it very clear that this is an unenforceable chapter, and it's just total mischief-making nonsense.

All right. I'm going to wrap- Finally, the racism directed towards Indians here is appalling. So in a final sentence- Right. Thank you... no democratic legitimacy. It's not been through a proper process, and the fact that I am an absolute expert in this, and get this- All right. We will need to wrap it up there.

Thank you. We appreciate your time- And it therefore has no social license and no treaty compliance... this morning. And that will bring your submission today to an end. Thank you. Okay, we move now to Ngā Toki Whakarururanga, who I believe are online with us as well, Donna Kerige.

Can you hear us, Donna? Sorry. Kia ora. Tēnā koutou katoa. Right. We have 10 minutes set down for your hearing today. We'll hand straight over to you. Okay. Kia ora. Tēnā koutou katoa. Thank you for the opportunity to present Ngā Toki Whakarururanga's feedback today to the hearing.

My name is Donna Kerige, and I'm a Kaihautū member of the leadership team for Ngā Toki Whakarururanga. Ngā Toki Whakarururanga was created under a mediation agreement signed with the Crown in 2020, to work with the Crown and its responsibilities under Te Tiriti and He Wakaputanga in the arena of trade policy, relations, and agreements.

The mediation and agreements objective was to ensure that Ngā Toki Whakarururanga has a genuine and meaningful influence on negotiations, such as those in the India and New Zealand Free Trade Agreement. Despite this, the negotiating text, as Jane just said, was not shared with Ngā Toki Whakarururanga on a confidential basis prior to its signing.

Ngā Toki Whakarururanga's treaty assessment of the agreement concludes that in addition to the flawed process, the final agreement is not treaty compliant. It has also failed to include a new best practice protection for the Treaty of Waitangi as co-designed between MFAT and Ngā Toki Whakarururanga.

In addition, this agreement, I believe, exposes the government and the business community to greater risks, and more importantly, it is a lost opportunity for the New Zealand Government to develop a trade template that reflects true partnership with both our trading partners and its own Treaty of Waitangi partner.

Ngā Toki Whakarururanga is not anti-trade. It is pro astute trading partnerships based on ethical, culturally grounded, commercially viable, and internationally credible trade agreements. Māori have a long history as successful traders and entrepreneurs. Historically, we have proven ourselves to be some of the best strategists the world has seen, not only in warfare but in trade. We have actively resisted control by others over our trade enterprises across generations, whether in agriculture, rongoā Māori, kai sovereignty, the care and protection of taonga

species, or international commerce. We have consistently affirmed that rangatiratanga includes economic sovereignty. Māori expect to work with and alongside others as an equal partner, be that with the New Zealand Government or global trading partners, advancing commercial opportunities for Aotearoa, including the strengthening and protecting of Māori intergenerational knowledge rights and responsibilities within economic spaces and in accordance with the Treaty.In 2025, Dr Jessica Hutchins and myself, both Kaihautū

for Ngā Tūki Whakaruruhanga, traveled to India to talk to farmers about seed and traditional knowledge sharing. We learned that we had a lot in common when it came to our respect for food sovereignty, environmental sustainability, and economic priorities. Matua Pita Tipene, co-convenor of Ngā Tūki Whakaruruhanga at the time, and I also met in Queenstown with the New Zealand and Indian trade officials, and we were really impressed by how much we had in common in terms of our views and our perspectives.

So it was a complete surprise to us when the text of the trade agreement and its failure to protect New Zealanders' rights under Te Tiriti o Waitangi and the unbalanced nature of the agreement was eventually shared a few months ago. To be absolutely honest, this agreement feels like a pre-election political stunt rather than a partnership between trade allies.

It is heavily weighted in the interests of India at New Zealanders' expense. It trades aspirational tariff reductions for significantly increased, virtually impossible, New Zealand investment in India with no reciprocal investment in New Zealand, and very specific access to New Zealand's knowledge economy, strengthening India's ability to compete with us in a global marketplace. It feels more like a take it or leave it deal from India than an astute business agreement between allies. New Zealand is at very real risk of selling the crown jewels for unachievable targets and

unaffordable weak protections, including protections associated with the government's prior commitment to Te Tiriti. To be blunt, the treaty is the only tool New Zealand has available to it to protect itself against economic cannibalism by predatory oligopolies in larger trading nations.

Aotearoa must protect itself, our natural resources, and the knowledge economy, including Mātauranga Māori, by strengthening treaty compliant frameworks in this and other global trade agreements. Enduring and mutually rewarding international trade agreements rely on transparency, mutual trust, reciprocity, and an equitable distribution of risks and rewards across parties.

When you read the fine print, this agreement appears to lack both transparency and a balanced distribution of risk and reward between the people of India and New Zealand. Not to mention an unacceptable risk through the failure to adequately protect and strengthen to the highest level possible Māori intergenerational knowledge and our rights and responsibilities agreed to by our ancestors, our mutual ancestors in Te Tiriti. This agreement undermines the agreement entered into by our ancestors to determine our own path. There is no effective recognition of the

constitutional status of Te Tiriti of Waitangi or the Crown's obligations in the agreement's text, or protection for measures that comply with Tiriti of Waitangi. The key for Māori to exercise rangatiratanga under this agreement will depend on various committees established under the agreement. It is imperative that Māori have the right representation at every level to protect our mutual long-term interest and aligned cultural values, but there is no provision for Māori involvement in those committees or their decisions.

It's not clear who the New Zealand government expects will invest the 34 billion in India over the next 15 years. Is there a commitment from the current or new exporters to meet this investment promise in return for tariff reductions on their products? How much do the companies benefit from these proposed reductions?

How much do they currently invest in offshore companies, and would it be feasible to expect them to shift a substantial portion of that offshore investment to India to meet this target? It is near impossible for New Zealand to deliver on this investment promise. New Zealand's investment abroad to all countries in 2025 was $1.5 billion. The math just doesn't stack up.

There does not appear to be a plan on how the government intend to address its current account deficit, giving this aspirational promise of $34 billion direct investment into India, especially when New Zealand citizens are repeatedly told that we must tighten our belts and that there are no lolly scrambles to be had. Well, at least here in New Zealand.

While India is a trading partner, nothing in this agreement prevents India from being a New Zealand competitor in global markets once we have shared the knowledge that makes us world leaders in certain agriculture, forestry, fisheries, film, and other industries we excel at. Armed with our expertise, India can leverage its economies of scale, wealth, and market size to effectively compete with New Zealand capability in the international trade space.

There doesn't appear to be any conditions in the agreement to encourage India to partner with New Zealand companies, including Māori enterprises, in the pursuit of new trading opportunities that leverage our, New Zealand's, existing knowledge capabilities and their economies of scales in new markets.

We also shouldn't convince ourselves that the proposed tariff cuts over the next decade will give us an advantage over others exporting into India. Our exporters will be competing in that market with many other countries that India has also negotiated FTAs with. They have much greater negotiating power and populations than New Zealand does.In conclusion, the treaty is the only tool New Zealand has available to protect itself against economic cannibalization from much larger trading nations and commercial enterprises. Aotearoa must protect itself, our

natural resources, and the knowledge economy, including Mātauranga Māori, by strengthening treaty-compliant frameworks within all trade agreements. Te Tiriti is a legitimate opportunity for the New Zealand Government to protect our knowledge economy, our natural resources, and our environment, nga taonga Māori, from exploitation.

We have seen what weak indigenous protection clauses and FDAs result in when we look at the impact on Mexico when their government's attempt to refuse the importation of GMO corn and was taken to task by the USA, who ultimately won their case despite their indigenous protection clause. This agreement fails to adequately protect the people of New Zealand as outlined in Te Tiriti including the traditional rights and responsibilities of Māori.

The agreement has the potential to collapse our economy if we are to honor our side of the agreement by investing 34 billion we can ill afford. And providing India with open access to our knowledge economy, enabling trading partners to compete with us in exchange for tariff reductions without any commitment to reciprocal investment in wholly owned New Zealand companies.

Ngā Tūki Whakaruruhanga is committed to shaping a system that is ethical, culturally grounded, commercially viable, and internationally credible in the international trade arena. It expects to help protect New Zealand from highly extractive models of trade that prioritize intellectual property capture and exploitation over community and environmental benefits.

New Zealand wants to become globally competitive in our areas of expertise. It will not come from scale alone. It will come from a combination of unique biodiversity, indigenous knowledge systems, ethical leadership, and trusted resources. We must protect those strengths while enabling innovation.

I have one more sentence, sorry. Ngā Tūki Whakaruruhanga do not support this agreement being passed into law without significant revision to protect the rights and responsibilities of the economic security and sustainability of New Zealand citizens as provided for in Te Tiriti o Waitangi and the treaty exception clause jointly agreed between Ngā Tūki and MFAT.
Okay. Kia ora. Thank you for your time. Thank you very much for your time. Cheers. Okay, we move now to our last submitter, Sam Brosnan.

My concerns today on the free trade agreement is just the speed it has come with and some effects that I think have been overlooked in certain industries where the industry I'm in, we're looking at a large influx of workers, definitely in the part-time sense.

And roughly it costs around $2,000 to train new staff across the board in many industries. So my concerns was, have we looked into staff training for these part-time students, and the IP loss we may incur at the end of their studies? That was my concerns.
And also the added costs through health and safety and ACC, I guess, that they're all entitled to through our work thing, sorry.

A second concern was obviously on transport, how we're going to monitor transportation.
